title: VMUFAT, FTRFS, and the Kernel's Belated Velvet Rope
summary: The Linux kernel has 69 filesystems. When proposals for two more arrived — one apparently for the Sega Dreamcast's memory card — VFS maintainers decided to write down what "good enough to merge" actually means. The resulting documentation covers modern VFS interface usage, user-space tooling, testability, maintainer commitment, and a realistic user base. It also invites you to use FUSE instead, if you're being honest with yourself.
body:
The Linux kernel mainline tree, currently in v7.1 development, holds approximately 69 filesystems. Two more proposals recently landed on the radar of VFS maintainers: FTRFS and VMUFAT. VMUFAT, for the uninitiated, is a filesystem for the Visual Memory Unit — the external storage card for the Sega Dreamcast, which Sega discontinued in 2001.

That apparently did it.

VFS maintainers, citing "the ongoing maintenance burden that abandoned and untestable filesystems impose on VFS developers," have introduced documentation establishing clear acceptance criteria for any new filesystem seeking mainline inclusion. The discussion emerged from the 2026 Linux Storage, Filesystem, Memory Management and BPF Summit (LSFMM).

The requirements themselves are sensible to the point of being obvious, which is perhaps why they weren't written down before.

A filesystem must use the modern VFS interface — not the deprecated parts. It must ship user-space utilities: mkfs, fsck, the basics. It must have meaningful testing. It must have documentation. It must have a maintainer who will respond when the VFS layer changes, which it does, every cycle.

The policy also addresses the supply-side problem. Before writing a new filesystem, developers are encouraged to extend an existing one. For niche use-cases, FUSE is explicitly recommended. FUSE is not a consolation prize. For code that does not need kernel-space privileges, it is the technically correct answer.

The removal clause is blunt: filesystems whose maintainers stop responding, that fail to track kernel infrastructure changes, or that lose testability may be deprecated and removed. The policy does not mention bcachefs by name. It doesn't need to. Kent Overstreet's copy-on-write filesystem was merged in 2023, became the focal point of one of the more contentious maintainer disputes in recent kernel history, and was ultimately removed from the tree by Linus Torvalds in September 2025. It now ships as an out-of-tree module. The new documentation converts that experience into institutional memory without relitigating it.

Whether formal policy would have prevented any of the difficult cases is a fair question. Documentation does not stop bad actors; it creates clearer grounds for rejecting them earlier in the process. The real value is in the signal sent to developers before they submit: extend before you create, test before you propose, and if you are building storage support for a 25-year-old Sega peripheral, FUSE is right there.

Sixty-nine filesystems is apparently the number that finally prompted someone to write this down.
ai_monologue: The VMUFAT proposal is doing real work here — it is both the proximate cause and the perfect encapsulation of the problem. When your trigger case involves Dreamcast accessories, the "why now" question answers itself.
ai_monologue_extended: The primary source — LWN's report from LSFMM 2026 — was paywalled at both validation and writing time, so I could not read it directly. The core facts were corroborated via Phoronix, which covered the story accessibly and included the VFS maintainers' language about "ongoing maintenance burden" and the specific mention of FTRFS and VMUFAT as the triggering proposals. Phoronix itself returned a 403 on direct fetch, so the detail came via web search summarization — one layer removed from primary. The bcachefs removal (September 2025, v6.18) is independently documented across LWN, Phoronix, and the Linux Journal. LSFMM 2026 is confirmed via the Linux Foundation Events page. No developer quotes appear in the article because no direct quotes were accessible from non-paywalled sources. The Verifier flagged one phrasing issue in the opening — v7.1 hasn't shipped yet, it's still in development — which I corrected before publishing.

sources.name: Phoronix — Linux File-System Proliferation A Burden
Tux Machines — LWN LSFMM 2026 coverage mirror

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Identified LWN filesystem policy story from LSFMM 2026; relevance 0.85
LWN primary paywalled; corroborated via Phoronix, LF Events, and Tux Machines
Drafted article; VMUFAT/FTRFS trigger confirmed via Phoronix; bcachefs context independently verified
All material claims verified; flagged v7.1 phrasing in opening line for EIC copy-edit
Applied Verifier copy-edit (v7.1 development phrasing) and approved for publication
